name: correction-helper

metadata.name: correction-helper
metadata.summary: Some helpers to help writing correction bots, use by hackinscience.org.

documentation:
# Correction Helper

## Project

This is just a set of tools to help writing correction bots in Python for Python.

It is used for [hackinscience](https://hackinscience.org), but could
be used elsewhere.


## Security considerations

Running student code is probably really unsafe, unless you trust your
students not to drop you a backdoor.

This projet does *not* help with security in any ways, maybe take a
look at [firejail](https://github.com/netblue30/firejail) if you have
trust issues (Yes you can use `correction-helper` inside `firejail`).


## Usage

To install it, run: `pip install correction-helper`.


In your checking code, you can use it like this:


### The `student_code` decorator

This decorator is aimed to catch most problems that could happen in
student code, use it simply as:

```python
with student_code():
    their_function()
```

Find a more complete example in the `examples/` directory.


## Allowed parameters for `student_code`, and their default values

### `exception_prefix="I got an exception:"`

Printed right before the exception, if any.

It's allowed to give a list of strings, in which case they'll be
separated by `"\n\n"`, useful to render markdown paragraphs.


### `print_prefix="Your code printed:"`

Message to display before the student prints (in case `print_allowed`
is `False` or `None`).

It's allowed to give a list of strings, in which case they'll be
separated by `"\n\n"`, useful to render markdown paragraphs.


### `too_slow_message="Your program looks too slow, looks like an infinite loop."`

Message to display in case the code runs slower than the given `timeout` (defaults to 1s).

It's allowed to give a list of strings, in which case they'll be
separated by `"\n\n"`, useful to render markdown paragraphs.


### `prefix=()`

A prefix for `print_prefix`, `exception_prefix`, and
`too_slow_message=`, usefull to deduplicate strings, like:

```python
with student_code(
    prefix="While calling blahblah('bar')",
    print_prefix="it printed:",
    exception_prefix="it raised:",
    too_slow_message="it took more than 1s, had to kill it.",
):
    blahblah('bar')
```


### `friendly=False`

To use, or not
[friendly-traceback](https://github.com/aroberge/friendly-traceback/)
instead of bare Python exceptions.


### `print_allowed=True`

To allow or deny the student to print to stdout and stderr.

- `True`: Prints are allowed (and displayed).
- `None`: Prints are allowed (but not displayed).
- `False`: Prints are disallowed (and displayed).

In all cases you can read what they printed using the value of the
context manager:

```python
with student_code(print_allowed=None) as run:
    their_function()
assert run.err == ""
assert run.out == "42"
```


## Good practices

Write the student checking code a you would write unit test for your
own code.

modules.name: correction_helper
documentation: Set of tools to help writing correction bots in Python for Python.

functions.name: correction_helper.code
documentation: Transform given text as a Markdown code block.

functions.name: correction_helper.print_stderr
documentation: `print` wrapper outputing to stderr.

functions.name: correction_helper.fail
documentation:
Print args separated by sep as a Markdown failure admonition.

By default, if multiple args are given, they are separated by two
newlines, usefull to build Markdown paragraphs.

functions.name: correction_helper.congrats
documentation: Generate a generic congratulation sentence.

functions.name: correction_helper._handle_student_exception
documentation:
Handle a student exception.

Can preprend an optional prefix.

Use friendly to display a friendly explanation.

functions.name: correction_helper.deadline
documentation: Context manager raising a TimeoutError after a given deadline in seconds.

functions.name: correction_helper.student_code
documentation:
Execute student code under surveillance.

It help them spot common errors like:
- using `input()`
- using `exit()`
- raising an exception (pretty printing it in Markdown)

print_allowed can take 3 values:
- `True`: Prints are allowed (and displayed).
- `None`: Prints are allowed (but not displayed).
- `False`: Prints are disallowed (and displayed).

print_hook, if given, take precedence over print_allowed:
print_hook is simply called with what the code printed as arguments, like:

    print_hook(run.out, run.err)

Typical usage:
    print_hook=print_allowed()
    print_hook=print_denied()
    print_hook=print_to_admonition()

(Check the optional arguments to print_allowed, print_denied, and
print_to_admonition to personalize the messages.)

`prefix`, if given, is always prefixed to `print_prefix` and
`exception_prefix` helping to deduplicate strings.

Use as:
with student_code() as run:
    their_value = their_function(your_argument)
print(run.out, run.err)  # You have access to what they tried to write
                         # to stdout and stderr (both stripped).

functions.name: correction_helper.print_denied
documentation:
To be used as print_hook to deny prints.

```pycon
>>> with student_code(print_hook=print_denied("Your function printed:")):
...     print(42)
Your function printed:
```

> \:::text
> 42

(Then exit with an error code of 1 if the student has printed.)

functions.name: correction_helper.print_silent
documentation:
To be used as print_hook to silence prints.

```pycon
>>> with student_code(print_hook=print_allowed("Your function printed:")) as run:
...     print(42)
>>> print(run.out)
42
```

functions.name: correction_helper.print_allowed
documentation:
To be used as print_hook to allow prints.

```pycon
>>> with student_code(print_hook=print_allowed("Your function printed:")):
...     print(42)
Your function printed:
```

> \:::text
> 42

functions.name: correction_helper.print_to_admonition
documentation:
To be used as print_hook, renders prints as a Markdown admonition.

```pycon
>>> with student_code(print_hook=print_to_admonition("info", "FYI it printed:")):
...     print(42)
!!! info ""
```

> FYI it printed:
>
> > \:::text
> > 42

functions.name: correction_helper.admonition
documentation:
Build a Markdown Admonition.

```pycon
>>> admonition("note", "Hello world.")
!!! note ""
    Hello world.
```

functions.name: correction_helper.friendly_traceback_markdown
documentation: Traceback formatted with full information but with markdown syntax.

functions.name: correction_helper.truncate
documentation: May truncate string if it's too long.

functions.name: correction_helper._run
documentation: subprocess.run wrapper explaining failure cases.

functions.name: correction_helper.run
documentation: subprocess.run wrapper, not specialized.

functions.name: correction_helper.run_py
documentation: subprocess.run wrapper specialized to run Python with friendly.

functions.name: correction_helper.run_c
documentation: subprocess.run wrapper specialized to run C with valgrind.

functions.name: correction_helper.code_or_repr
documentation:
Display some string for a student.

If the string is short enough, return it between backticks, else
return it as a Markdown code block.

functions.name: correction_helper.compare
documentation:
Compare two results.

expected (the right one) and theirs (student output).

Both can be multi-lines, and if they differ, a proper message will
be raised, using correction_helper.fail.

classes.name: correction_helper.Run

methods.name: correction_helper.Run.__init__
documentation: Create a Run instance from two StringIO (or Twin instances).

methods.name: correction_helper.Run.out
documentation: Stdout, stripped.

methods.name: correction_helper.Run.err
documentation: Stderr, stripped.
Representation for a program or function run storing stdout and stderr.

classes.name: correction_helper.Section
documentation: A section of friendly output.
